Subject: Unexpected Failure of Data Processing System Today
Body:
Hello Customer Support,
I am contacting you to report an unexpected failure of the data processing system. There was no prior warning and I was not able to access any data. I believe the failure could be related to an incompatibility with the Node.js version update. I have already restarted my computer and double-checked the Smart Home Sensor connections, but the problem still exists. Could you please investigate and provide a solution as soon as possible? Please inform me if you require further details from me. Thank you for your time and assistance.

Body: Food Crawl Standout office lunches within the PATH ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ FOOD CRAWL By Karon Liu My colleagues and I recently explored how hybrid work has changed downtown Toronto. I wrote about how the lunchtime crowd is slowly returning to the city’s core, and Katie Daubs looked into the state of the PATH, the city’s underground labyrinth that spans more than 30 kilometres. It boasts lots of food options — so if you’ve returned to work downtown, here are some great spots for lunch or a midday treat. FIRST STOP Karon Liu/Toronto Star The teriyaki tofu from Ho Ga Oriental Cuisine Ho Ga Oriental Cuisine is one of the few independent food stalls in the PATH. It’s in one of the smaller food courts, but it’s hardly a secret. Even on quiet days, there’s a line down the hall because this longstanding Korean-Japanese spot offers lunch boxes that cost less than $9. Due to the pandemic, the menu has been pared down to seven items, but it’s still a solid roster of staples like bulgogi and teriyaki. The teriyaki tofu is a hefty meal: four slabs of firm tofu in a thick batter with a light teriyaki sauce, served over steamed rice with a choice of two sides. I opted for the kimchi slaw — it’s slightly astringent, but less spicy than regular kimchi — and jap chae, Korean glass noodles. It's a comforting home-cooked meal that's the closest you'll get to bringing a packed lunch. WHERE TO GET IT Ho Ga is in the 121 King St. W. building (just east of York Street) in the lower level. Like all food stalls in the PATH, it’s only open on weekdays. It’s a small mom-and-pop operation, so it’s not on delivery apps. Need a trusty guide through the housing crunch? Living Here, the Star’s free newsletter, can help. Each week, it’ll bring you the analysis and expert advice you need to navigate renting, buying, owning or just getting by in and around Toronto — plus more of the Star’s essential real estate coverage. Sign up for free here. NOW TRY Karon Liu/Toronto Star The Oaxaca bowl from Kupfert and Kim When Kupfert and Kim opened its first location a decade ago, my coworkers were ecstatic about having a fully vegan lunch spot in the downtown core. Since day one, I’ve ordered the Oaxaca bowl: a bed of brown rice, shredded kale, black beans, cashew crème, pico de gallo, guacamole, sliced radish, beets, tortilla chips and lime wedges. It’s the ideal office worker lunch — it’s filling, full of vegetables and has a nice balance of protein and carbs to get you through the day without wanting a nap. The cashew crème is a nutty, creamy sauce that pairs well with the nutty brown rice, while the acidity in the pico de gallo helps cut the kale’s bitterness. The bowl costs $14.75, which is on par for a PATH food court lunch nowadays, but in terms of size and number of ingredients, it’s one of the better value meals. WHERE TO GET IT Kupfert and Kim has multiple locations in the PATH. They each have different hours — some are open until 10 p.m. All the locations have the same menu, but my favourite location is in the RBC Waterpark Place upstairs food court at 20 Bay St. (at Queens Quay West). The food court has reliable free WiFi and lots of natural light, and it’s close to Scotiabank Arena. You can find a directory of all locations and order online on its website. Karon Liu/Toronto Star The classic peppercorn steak from Jump Restaurant Jump has been a reliable lunch and dinner spot for decades. Though it may seem better suited for high-powered execs with money to spend, the menu prices are a little more affordable than you’d expect for what you’re getting (especially for a fine-dining restaurant in the heart of the Financial District). At $36, the 8 oz peppercorn sirloin coulotte — a lean cut from the sirloin tip — with frites is a reasonable lunch splurge. If you’re here for dinner, the alternative is a $52 7 oz tenderloin. My medium-rare steak was cooked perfectly, and the green peppercorn jus added a rich astringent contrast. The fries are delicately crisp, and it also comes with a side of grilled broccolini. WHERE TO GET IT Jump is on the street level of Commerce Court at 18 Wellington St. W. (right between Yonge and Bay streets). Reservations are highly recommended during the weekday lunch rush between noon and 2 p.m. — otherwise you can try your luck at the bar. There isn’t a strict dress code, but the crowd is all navy suits so you might want to at least go for business casual attire. Karon Liu/Toronto Star The snickerdoodle cookie from Maman During the mid-afternoon coffee runs you’ll see a flood of office workers clutching blue and white cups from Maman, this New York-based café and bakery. The lattes are on par with the third-wave espresso bars you can find elsewhere in the core, but it’s the cookies that are worth coming back for. The snickerdoodle one is made with plant-based butter, making it one of the rare vegan baked goods in the PATH that isn’t the typical coconut-date concoction. It has a crisp exterior rolled in cinnamon sugar, while the inside is chewy and moist. The cookies are sized quite generously, so one is big enough to nibble on throughout the day, and it costs $4.50. WHERE TO GET IT Maman is in First Canadian Place’s upstairs food court at 100 King St. W., between Bay and York streets. It’s open on weekdays from 8 a.m. to around 2:30 p.m. Its hours are on its website, and you can order online through Uber Eats. Thanks for reading. If you haven’t seen it yet, here’s my alternative Michelin Guide featuring 15 must-eat restaurants in Toronto, and here’s the Toronto-area restaurants that made this year’s list of Canada’s 100 Best Restaurants. I’ll see you back in your inbox next Thursday. Subject: [forteana] Fwd: Explorator 5.15
Body: ================================================================explorator 5.15 August 15, 2002================================================================Editor's note: Depending on your mail software, some urls maywrap (especially those from the Telegraph) which will requireyou to rebuild the url at your end; if you get a 'file notfound', check to see if the url wrapped on you. Most urls shouldbe active for at least eight hours from the time of publication.================================================================================================================================Thanks to Bill Thayer, Ross Sargent, 'Salinas17', Michael Ruggieri,Maurice O'Sullivan, Terry Gibson, 'mb2400', Bill Kennedy, Paul JamesCowie, Arthur Shippee, Joseph Nicholas, W. Richard Frahm,'alesmonetos',and Sally Winchester for headses upses this week (a.a.h.i.h.l.n.o.o.)n.b. Last week I carelessly suggested the team which had claimedto have found El Dorado was Polish; it is, in fact, an internationalteam led by a "Polish-Italian" journalist/explorer. Apologies forany confusion this may have caused.================================================================================================================================AFRICA, EUROPE, AND ASIA================================================================John Noble Wilford attempts to clarify the Chadian skull thing: the what-can-DNA-tell-us-about-antiquity department, the latestsuggestion is that there is genetic evidence "middle easter farmers'civilised' Europe": log boat which might date to 3000 B.C. has been found off thecoast of Ireland: Cerne Abbas Giant was recently, er, 'defaced' (not permanently ...potentially offensive content in this one): technology has revealed a prehistoric site nearNorthumberland: and a prehistoric cemetery has been found near Cheshire: still making significant finds in the 'workers village'nearthe pyramids: News has a nice feature on the alignment of a Dilmun temple atSaar: of coverage of a story on Bronze Age 'drug trade': Egyptian Museum in Cairo will be marking its centennial byhauling up a bunch of never-before-seen artifacts from the basement: piece on politics and archaeology in the Holy Land: while archaeologists seem to be quietly excavating a Byzantinemonastery in Gaza: first century cemetery has come to light near Palmyra: human-size statue of Baal Addir was recently revealed in a Sardiniancontext: Celtic Iron Age fort in Wales is "revealing its secrets": large number of Eastern Zhou Dynasty tombs have been found inChina: as has an important tomb of an aristocrat: Buddhas have been found hidden inside another Buddha rarelyseen by the public: is in danger of becoming extinct: a battle brewing over the fate of the "Welsh Mary Rose": AMERICAS================================================================There is evidence of pre-Columbian dentistry in Mexico: found near Lake Jackson (Texas) might be 11,000 years old: nice report on excavations near Fort Vancouver: construction in Virginia has revealed the grave of a slave: THE NEWSSTANDS================================================================A new issue of Bible Review, with online articles on a collectionof New Testament manuscripts, the Exodus debate, and other things: and Archaeology Odyssey, with articles on the ten most endangeredarchaeological sites, Moses Shapira (wow), et alia: THE WEB================================================================I came across the Into Archaeology website this week; it's worth a look for a number of reasons, but in particular its articles sectionhas some good stuff (currently, an interview with Adrienne Mayor, apieceon the Thera ships, and a piece on the work at Bird Cove): Oriental Institute has recently added a whole pile of publically-accessible articles to its News and Notes section: ONLINE BOOKS================================================================George Dennis, *Cities and Cemeteries of Etruria*: *Odes and Carmen Saeculare* (Conington trans.): *Satires, Epistles, and Ars Poetica* (Conington trans.): THE ARCHAEOLOGY CHANNEL================================================================Calleva: The Roman Town of Silchester: BEAT================================================================There are claims that recent Al-Qaeda casualties were actuallypotential antiquity thieves: ABOUT.COM================================================================... can't seem to connect today (hmmmmm...)================================================================REVIEWS================================================================Iain Pears, *Dream of Scipio*: Perrottet, *Route 66 A.D.: On the Trail of Ancient Roman Tourists* Powell, * Athens and Sparta: Constructing Greek political andsocial history from 478 BC* (Epidavros): from a Lost Civilization (Toronto): CORNER================================================================Famed debunker James Randi has been running a series on Lucian'sdebunking of Alexander of Abonutichus which is worth a look. It's in four parts (you'll have to skim through some other, readable,stuff to get to the bits on Lucian ... it tends to be near the end): case you meet someone who asks, "What's all the hubbub about thoseElgin Marbles?", here's a nice article to hand them: precedents cited in a piece on gossip: content in a story of a man with way too much time on his hands: not to pursue a classics degree: guy is studying why ancient Greek buildings seem to do so wellin earthquakes (do they?): reader alerted me to LewRockwell.com, this week, which is a politicalsite which at least one item of interest ... a piece by DanielMcCarthy on Greek ideas of liberty: of ClassCon in a Newsweek piece "Why We Need Heaven": and a reason for studying Latin and Greek in an interview withDavid McCollough: sure whether there's comparanda potential in this bit on the crowning of King Puck: Jones in the Spectator (there's a Hannibal movie coming!): and Dot Wordsworth: Harris' Classics Corner: Finland's Nuntii Latini Bremen's Der Monatsrückblick - auf Latein Weather in Latin: Chinese Mail: Bricks: Nazi Gold": (the coverage varies quite a bit in these): Beer: Chess Piece: Egyptian Tombs: of Ramses I: of Roman sculpture by Princeton: Plane from York: Map: is a weekly newsletter representing the fruits ofthe labours of 'media research division' of The Atrium. Variouson-line news and magazine sources are scoured for news of theancient world (broadly construed: practically anything relatingto archaeology or history prior to about 1700 or so is fairgame) and every Sunday they are delivered to your mailbox free ofcharge!================================================================Useful Addresses================================================================Past issues of Explorator are available on the web at: subscribe to Explorator, send a blank email message to:mailto:Explorator-subscribe@yahoogroups.comTo unsubscribe, send a blank email message to:mailto:Explorator-unsubscribe@yahoogroups.comTo send a 'heads up' to the editor:mailto:dmeadows@idirect.com================================================================Explorator is Copyright (c) 2002 David Meadows. Feel free todistribute these listings via email to your pals, students,teachers, etc., but please include this copyright notice. Subject: Available payment methods for multiple subscriptions
Body: I am looking to subscribe to multiple products. Could you inform me about the payment options that are available? Specifically, can I make payments using a credit card or by bank transfer? Are there any special deals for large orders?

Subject: Report on Malfunctioning Data Analytics Tools
Body: Greetings Customer Support, I hope this message finds you well. I am writing to bring to your attention an issue we have been experiencing with our data analytics tools. These tools have been malfunctioning, causing significant disruptions to our operations. Upon investigation, we discovered that the issue stems from outdated software versions. Despite restarting the systems and checking the licenses, the problem has not resolved. We kindly request an update on the appropriate software versions or guidance on how to address this issue. Additionally, any other information you might need from us to facilitate the resolution would be greatly appreciated. Thank you for your assistance and we look forward to your prompt response.

Subject: Re: API Monitoring Dashboard Suggestion
Body:
Dear John,

Thank you for reaching out and for the valuable feedback! We appreciate you taking the time to share your suggestion.

We agree that a customizable widget for displaying average response times would be a beneficial addition to our API monitoring dashboard. We've added your request to our feature backlog and will be reviewing it in our upcoming development cycle.

While we can't provide an exact timeline for implementation, we'll keep you updated on our progress.

In the meantime, please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any other questions or suggestions.

Best regards,
Aetheros Support Team
